---
title: Week 6 Homework
subtitle: Use of Color in Graphs 
format: html
editor: 
  markdown: 
    wrap: 72
---

## Introduction

In this assignment, you will recreate your first New York Times Data
Visualization independently. The goal is not just to copy a graph, but
to practice how we build visualizations step by step using the concepts
we've learned in class, especially color mapping, scales, and core
`ggplot2` functions.  

 

You will take a real-world dataset and transform it into a meaningful
visualization by thinking carefully about:

-   how variables are mapped to visual elements (like position, color,
    and size)
-   how color is used to represent different types of data and
-   how small design choices impact interpretation.

By the end of this assignment, you should feel more comfortable reading,
modifying, and creating layered visualizations in R.

## The Article

This assignment is inspired by a data visualization from The New York
Times that explores global inequalities in COVID-19 vaccine
distribution. The graphic compares countries based on wealth (GDP per
capita) and vaccination rates, while also incorporating additional
context like income groups and population.

 

Your task is to recreate a version of this visualization using real
data.

## The Graphic

The data visualization of this module is based on the following article
(by Keith Collins and Josh Holder) of The New York Times (Dec 9, 2021):

**What Data Shows About Vaccine Supply and Demand in the Most Vulnerable
Places**

<https://www.nytimes.com/interactive/2021/12/09/world/vaccine-inequity-supply.html>

<https://www.nytimes.com/2022/01/06/learning/whats-going-on-in-this-graph-jan-12-2022.html>

Specifically, we are interested in replicating the following graphic:

![](images/covid-vaccionation-doses-and-income-gdp.png)

## The Data:

You will work with multiple real-world datasets from sources such as the
World Bank and Our World in Data. These datasets include information on:

-   GDP per capita

-   COVID-19 vaccination rates

-   country income groups

-   and population

There are 4 main sources of data:

-   **GDP per capita** from the *World Bank* <br>
    <https://data.worldbank.org/indicator/NY.GDP.PCAP.CD>

-   **Vaccination data** from *Our World in Data* <br>
    <https://ourworldindata.org/search?q=vaccine%20doses>

-   **World Bank income groups, 1987 to 2022** from *Our World in Data*
    <br>
    <https://ourworldindata.org/grapher/world-bank-income-groups?tab=table>

-   **Population by Country** from the *World Bank* <br>
    <https://data.worldbank.org/indicator/SP.POP.TOTL>

## Part 1 - Setup

```{r}
# load the necessary packages

library(tidyverse)
library(lubridate)
```

```{r}
# load GDP data
# Note: skip = 4 skips the first 4 rows of metadata before the actual data begins
dat_gdp = read_csv("API_NY.GDP.PCAP.CD_DS2_en_csv_v2_557863.csv", skip = 4)

# load vaccination data
# Note: skip = 1 skips the default header row so we can assign our own column names
# col_types ensures Date is parsed correctly for use with lubridate
dat_vac = read_csv(
  "covid-vaccination-doses-per-capita.csv",
  skip = 1,
  col_names = c("Entity", "Code", "Date", "Vaccinations"),
  col_types = "ccDd"
)

# load income group data
# Note: skip = 1 for the same reason as above
dat_inc = read_csv(
  "world-bank-income-groups.csv",
  col_names = c("Entity", "Code", "Year", "Income"),
  col_types = "ccdc",
  skip = 1
)

# load population data
# Note: skip = 4 for the same reason as the GDP file
dat_pop = read_csv("API_SP.POP.TOTL_DS2_en_csv_v2_665226.csv", skip = 4)
```

## Part 2 - Preparing GDP Data

Select only the columns you need:

-   Country Name

-   Country Code

-   2021 GDP

```{.r}
dat_gdp_2021 = select(dat_gdp, ________, ________, ________)
```

*Hint: GDP for 2021 is stored in a column named `2021`. You can
reference it with backticks: `` `2021` ``.*

## Part 3 - Filter vaccination data

Filter the vaccination data down to the year in question (2021). We want
the most recent recorded value for each country in that year.

```{.r}
dat_vac_2021 = dat_vac |>
  filter(!is.na(Code)) |>
  mutate(Year = year(Date),
         Month = month(Date),
         Day = day(Date)) |>
  filter(Year == ________) |>
  group_by(Entity) |>
  slice_max(Month) |>
  slice_max(Day) |>
  ungroup()
```

*Hint: `slice_max(Month)` and `slice_max(Day)` keep the latest available
entry per country within 2021.*

## Part 4 - Merge GDP and Vaccination datasets

Once we have the GDP data and the vaccination data, the next step
involves merging them with `inner_join()`. This step is given to you
because we have not covered joins in this course — essentially it
combines two datasets into one by matching on a shared column.

```{.r}
gdp_vac = inner_join(dat_gdp_2021, dat_vac_2021, by = c("Country Code" = "Code"))

gdp_vac
```

## Part 5 - Prepare and merge Income Group data

Now we need to add income group labels to our dataset. The code below
filters the income data to 2021, cleans up the labels, and merges it in.
This step is also given to you.

```{.r}
# filter to 2021
dat_inc_2021 = filter(dat_inc, Year == 2021)

# clean up income labels (remove "-income countries" suffix)
dat_inc_2021$Income = str_remove(dat_inc_2021$Income, "-income countries")

# make Income an ordered factor so it displays correctly in plots
dat_inc_2021$Income = factor(
  x = dat_inc_2021$Income,
  levels = c("Low", "Lower-middle", "Upper-middle", "High"),
  ordered = TRUE
)

# merge with gdp_vac
gdp_vac_inc = inner_join(gdp_vac, dat_inc_2021, by = c("Country Code" = "Code"))

gdp_vac_inc
```

## Part 6 - Prepare and merge Population data

Finally, we add population data so we can size each point by country
population.

```{.r}
# select Country Name, Country Code, and the 2021 population column
dat_pop_2021 = select(dat_pop, c(1:2, 66))
colnames(dat_pop_2021) = c("Country Name", "Country Code", "Population")

# merge with gdp_vac_inc
gdp_vac_inc_pop = inner_join(gdp_vac_inc, dat_pop_2021, by = "Country Code")

gdp_vac_inc_pop
```

## Part 7 - Graphing

We will go over a series of plotting rounds, starting with a default
scatter plot, and then gradually adding more elements, and customizing
its appearance to get as close as possible to our target visualization.
 

a.  Create your first scatterplot using GDP and vaccination data  

You are mapping:  

-   GDP (wealth of a country) → x-axis

-   Vaccinations → y-axis

```{.r}
plot_a <- gdp_vac |> 
    ggplot(aes(x = ________, y = ________)) +
  geom_point()
```

b.  Improve the x-axis  

Make the plot easier to read by applying a log transformation to GDP, as
GDP varies a lot across countries. Log scales help us visualize large
differences more clearly.

```{.r}
plot_b <- gdp_vac |> 
    ggplot(aes(x = log(________, base = ________),
           y = Vaccinations)) +
  geom_point()
```

c.  Add labels  

Make your plot understandable by labeling the axes.

```{.r}
plot_c <- plot_b + labs(
  x = "________",
  y = "________"
)
```

d.  Add income groups (color!)  

Add a categorical variable using color to map Income groups. This
variable includes categories like Low, High, etc.

```{.r}
plot_d <- gdp_vac_inc |> 
    ggplot(aes(x = log(`2021`, base = 100),
           y = Vaccinations,
           color = ________)) +
  geom_point()
```

e.  Customize colors  

Apply a custom color palette to control how colors appear in the chart.
The `income_colors` vector below uses colors inspired by the original
NYT graphic — feel free to experiment with ColorBrewer palettes as well!

```{.r}
income_colors = c(
  rgb(242, 129, 36,  maxColorValue = 255),  # Low
  rgb(249, 199, 158, maxColorValue = 255),  # Lower-middle
  rgb(166, 197, 198, maxColorValue = 255),  # Upper-middle
  rgb(54,  123, 127, maxColorValue = 255)   # High
)

plot_e <- plot_d + scale_color_manual(values = income_colors)
```

f.  Add population (size)  

Add another variable to the plot. You are mapping population to the size
of each point.

```{.r}
plot_f <- gdp_vac_inc_pop |>
    ggplot(aes(x = log(`2021`, base = 100),
           y = Vaccinations,
           color = Income,
           size = ________)) +
  geom_point()
```

*Hint: The size of each bubble should represent the population of the
country.*

g.  Adjust point sizes  

Make sizes visually reasonable: scale population so the points don't
become too large.

```{.r}
plot_g <- ____

```

*Hint: use `geom_point(size = sqrt(Population / 10000000))` instead of
the default `geom_point()`*

h.  Final touches  

Improve the layout by adjusting where the legend appears. Look at the
original NYT graph to identify where the legend should appear.

```{.r}
plot_h <- _____
```

*Hint: use `+ theme(legend.position = "________")`*

## Part 8 - Reflection Questions

**1. Why is color used for Income instead of a numeric variable?**    

Your response:

  **2. Why is GDP shown on a log scale?**    

Your response:

  **3. What does the size of each point represent?**    

Your response:
